section.title: The Ark Brought to Jerusalem
section.sectionRationale: This chapter is the corrected sequel to chapter 13's failed attempt. David now does the careful, humbling work of consulting the Law: the Levites are gathered, consecrated, and, this time, carry the ark on their shoulders with poles, exactly as Moses commanded, rather than on a cart. Singers and musicians are appointed, sacrifices are offered, and the ark comes up to Jerusalem amid great rejoicing, only for Michal to despise David's uninhibited worship. For the Chronicler's original post-exilic readers, rebuilding right worship after generations of exile, this is a founding lesson in proper Levitical order.
section.thesis: When Israel seeks the LORD according to His own revealed order, carried by consecrated hands on His terms rather than human convenience, worship becomes what it was always meant to be: joyful, musical, sacrificial, and safe, and even the one voice of contempt in the chapter, Michal's, only highlights how right and good this corrected obedience truly is.
section.frameNote: Six beats: preparing to move the ark rightly, verses 1 to 3; the Levites gathered, verses 4 to 13; the Levites carry the ark on their shoulders, verses 14 to 16; singers and musicians appointed, verses 17 to 24; the ark comes up with rejoicing, verses 25 to 28; and Michal's contempt, verse 29.

units.label: Preparing to move the ark rightly
units.note: no one but the Levites may carry the ark

verses.text: David constructed buildings for himself in the City of David, and he prepared a place for the ark of God and pitched a tent for it.
Then David said, “No one but the Levites may carry the {{aron|ark}} of God, because the LORD has chosen them to {{nasa|carry}} the ark of the LORD and to {{sharat|minister}} before Him forever.”
And David assembled all Israel in Jerusalem to bring up the ark of the LORD to the place he had prepared for it.

units.label: The Levites gathered
units.note: it was because you Levites were not with us the first time

verses.text: Then he gathered together the descendants of Aaron and the Levites:
From the Kohathites, Uriel the chief and 120 of his relatives;
from the Merarites, Asaiah the chief and 220 of his relatives;
from the Gershomites, Joel the chief and 130 of his relatives;
from the Elizaphanites, Shemaiah the chief and 200 of his relatives;
from the Hebronites, Eliel the chief and 80 of his relatives;
and from the Uzzielites, Amminadab the chief and 112 of his relatives.
David summoned the priests Zadok and Abiathar and the Levites Uriel, Asaiah, Joel, Shemaiah, Eliel, and Amminadab.

And he said to them, “You are the heads of the Levitical families. You and your relatives must {{qadash|consecrate}} yourselves so that you may bring the ark of the LORD, the God of Israel, to the place I have prepared for it.
It was because you Levites were not with us the first time that the LORD our God burst forth in anger against us. For we did not consult Him about the proper order.”

units.label: The Levites carry the ark on their shoulders

verses.text: So the priests and Levites {{qadash|consecrated}} themselves to bring up the ark of the LORD, the God of Israel.
And the Levites {{nasa|carried}} the ark of God on their shoulders with the poles, as Moses had commanded in accordance with the word of the LORD.
David also told the leaders of the Levites to appoint their relatives as singers to lift up their voices with joy, accompanied by musical instruments, harps, lyres, and cymbals.
So the Levites appointed Heman son of Joel; from his brothers, Asaph son of Berechiah; from their brothers the Merarites, Ethan son of Kushaiah;
and with them their brothers next in rank: Zechariah, Jaaziel, Shemiramoth, Jehiel, Unni, Eliab, Benaiah, Maaseiah, Mattithiah, Eliphelehu, Mikneiah, and the gatekeepers Obed-edom and Jeiel.
The musicians Heman, Asaph, and Ethan were to sound the bronze cymbals.
Zechariah, Aziel, Shemiramoth, Jehiel, Unni, Eliab, Maaseiah, and Benaiah were to play the harps according to Alamoth.
And Mattithiah, Eliphelehu, Mikneiah, Obed-edom, Jeiel, and Azaziah were to lead the music with lyres according to Sheminith.
Chenaniah the head Levite was the director of the music because he was highly skilled.
Berechiah and Elkanah were to be guardians of the ark.
Shebaniah, Joshaphat, Nethanel, Amasai, Zechariah, Benaiah, and Eliezer, the priests, were to blow the trumpets before the ark of God. Obed-edom and Jehiah were also to be guardians of the ark.

units.label: The ark comes up with rejoicing
units.note: with shouting, rams' horns, and trumpets

verses.text: So David, the elders of Israel, and the commanders of thousands went with rejoicing to bring the ark of the covenant of the LORD from the house of Obed-edom.
And because God helped the Levites who were carrying the ark of the covenant of the LORD, they sacrificed seven bulls and seven rams.

Now David was dressed in a robe of fine linen, as were all the Levites who were carrying the ark, as well as the singers and Chenaniah, the director of music for the singers. David also wore a linen ephod.
So all Israel brought up the ark of the covenant of the LORD with shouting, with the sounding of rams' horns and trumpets, and with cymbals and the music of harps and lyres.

units.label: Michal's contempt
units.note: she despised him in her heart

verses.text: As the ark of the covenant of the LORD was entering the City of David, Saul's daughter Michal looked down from a window and saw King David dancing and celebrating, and she despised him in her heart.

aron.gloss: Ark or chest, the ark of the covenant, God's throne on earth. Chapter 13 showed what happens when it is handled wrongly; this chapter shows Israel finally learning to approach it as the Law required.

nasa.gloss: To carry, bear, or lift up. Here the Levites literally carry, nasa, the ark on their shoulders with the poles, exactly as Moses had commanded, Numbers 7:9, correcting chapter 13's fatal use of a cart. The same root described the LORD 'exalting,' nasa, David's kingdom in chapter 14; whether lifting a king or bearing God's presence, the verb points to what only God authorizes.

qadash.gloss: To consecrate, set apart as holy. From the same root as qodesh, holiness. David insists that the priests and Levites qadash themselves before touching or carrying the ark, verses 12 and 14, a direct correction of the carelessness that led to Uzzah's death; approaching a holy God requires being made holy first.

sharat.gloss: To minister or serve, especially in sacred service. The LORD chose the Levites to carry the ark and to sharat before Him forever, verse 2, language that will describe the ongoing musical ministry David establishes before the ark in the next chapter.

basic.context: After the disaster of chapter 13, David does not simply try again with more enthusiasm; he does his homework. He prepares a proper place and tent for the ark in Jerusalem, then gathers the priests and Levites, the specific families responsible under the Law for the sacred furnishings, and tells them plainly what went wrong the first time: 'we did not consult Him about the proper order' (verse 13). This time the Levites consecrate themselves and carry the ark on their shoulders using the poles, exactly as Moses commanded, never touching it directly. Singers and musicians are appointed by name, under Chenaniah's skilled direction, and the whole procession moves to Jerusalem with sacrifice, robes, shouting, trumpets, and joyful music. Only one sour note intrudes: Michal, Saul's daughter, watches David's uninhibited dancing and despises him in her heart, a contempt for wholehearted worship that the chapter leaves hanging as a foil to everything that has gone right.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative, and its message is stated by the narrative itself, not merely implied: verse 13 explicitly names the earlier failure as a failure to 'consult Him about the proper order,' and this chapter is the deliberate, careful correction. For the Chronicler's first readers, returning from exile and rebuilding worship almost from scratch, the message could not be more practical: God cares about how He is worshiped, not merely that He is worshiped, and getting the order right, consecrated people, proper carrying, appointed music, brings joy rather than judgment.
basic.originalLanguages: The ark, the aron, is finally carried, nasa, the way God intended, on the Levites' shoulders by its poles, not on a cart. Before they may carry it, the priests and Levites must qadash themselves, be consecrated, set apart as holy for holy work, a direct answer to the carelessness of chapter 13. And the Levites are described as those chosen to sharat, minister, before the ark forever, language of ongoing sacred service that flows straight into the appointment of singers and musicians who will minister continually before God's presence.
basic.typology: The Levites, consecrated before they may bear the ark, picture the truth that no one comes near God's holiness on unconsecrated terms; access requires being set apart. Their careful, joyful, musical service before the ark anticipates the ministry of praise that belongs, in Christ, to all believers as a royal priesthood, and ultimately points to Christ Himself, our great High Priest, perfectly consecrated and perfectly fit to bear God's presence to His people.
basic.christ: Where chapter 13 showed that no one may presume to bring God's presence near on their own terms, this chapter shows what it looks like when God's appointed way is followed: consecrated servants, careful obedience, and overflowing joy. This anticipates Christ, our great High Priest, who alone is truly and perfectly consecrated to bear God's presence to His people without danger, and who now welcomes all who trust Him into that same priestly nearness, 1 Peter 2:5, 9, offering the sacrifice of praise continually, Hebrews 13:15.
basic.god: God is holy and must be approached according to His own revealed order, yet that same God delights to bless obedience with overflowing joy, music, sacrifice, and celebration rather than fear. The chapter shows both truths together: the same LORD who struck down Uzzah for careless handling now fills a rightly ordered procession with gladness, because His holiness was never meant to exclude joy, only to direct it rightly.
basic.crossReferences: Numbers 4:15 and 7:9 command that the Kohathites carry the holy things by poles, on their shoulders, never touching them directly, the very instruction finally obeyed here. 2 Samuel 6:12-19 records this same procession with additional detail. Hebrews 13:15 speaks of offering to God a continual sacrifice of praise, echoing the Levites' appointed ministry of music.
basic.oneStory: This chapter is the corrected sequel to the ark's failed first journey: Israel learns to seek God's presence on His own terms, and the result is not judgment but overflowing worship, music, sacrifice, and joy. For the returning exiles rebuilding right worship, and for every reader since, it teaches that careful obedience to how God has said He is to be approached is the doorway to the deepest joy, not an obstacle to it, a joy fulfilled in Christ, who welcomes His people into continual, confident praise.

context.literary: This chapter directly answers chapter 13, repeating its subject, moving the ark to Jerusalem, but reversing its outcome through careful obedience. The Chronicler devotes unusual space to naming the Levitical families, singers, and musicians (verses 4-24), a deliberate slowing of the narrative that shows how seriously the Chronicler, writing for a community rebuilding temple worship, takes proper Levitical order; the joyful procession (verses 25-28) is the reward of that patience, and Michal's contempt (verse 29) is a single, isolated note left to stand as contrast.
context.historical: The Kohathites, Merarites, and Gershonites named here are the three Levitical clans given specific transport duties for the tabernacle furnishings in Numbers 3-4 and 7; David's careful summons of each clan's chief shows he is now following the Mosaic assignment of duties precisely. Sacrificing seven bulls and seven rams (verse 26) after six steps of safe progress is recorded in the parallel account, 2 Samuel 6:13, as an act of relief and thanksgiving that the ark could now be carried without disaster.
context.canonical: For the post-exilic community rebuilding the temple and its worship after generations without it, this chapter's exhaustive attention to proper Levitical order, who carries what, who sings, who plays, who guards, was not antiquarian detail but a template: it showed exactly how Israel's worship, once neglected and then bungled, could be restored rightly, under God's own instructions, to the joy it was always meant to carry.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show, in careful and deliberate detail, exactly how Israel corrected its earlier failure to seek God according to His revealed order, so that later generations, rebuilding worship after exile, would have a clear model of consecrated, obedient, joyful worship.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter's specific arrangements, which Levitical clan carries what, which musicians play which instrument, were tied to Israel's Mosaic covenant and temple worship and are not binding prescriptions for how believers must worship today. What does transfer as abiding truth is the underlying principle: worship must be offered according to what God has revealed, in consecrated, careful obedience, and such obedience leads to real joy rather than restricting it.

debates.question: Why does the Chronicler give so much space to naming musicians and their instruments?

views.label: To establish the legitimacy of the later temple musical order
views.case: The named singers and their descendants, especially Asaph, Heman, and Ethan, become the very Levitical musical families who serve for generations in the temple, 1 Chronicles 25:1-31; naming them here roots that entire institution in David's own careful, Law-obedient establishment of worship.

views.label: To show that no detail of worship is beneath God's care
views.case: By naming individuals down to who guards the ark and who plays which instrument in which mode, the text teaches that faithful worship is not a vague spiritual feeling but a matter of real people doing real, specific, well-ordered work, none of it too small to matter to God.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the detail is purposeful, not padding; it establishes both a lasting institution and a lasting principle about the seriousness and specificity of ordered worship.

debates.question: How should readers understand Michal's contempt in verse 29?

views.label: Contempt rooted in wounded royal pride
views.case: As Saul's daughter, Michal may have seen David's uninhibited, unrobed dancing before the people as beneath royal dignity, an embarrassment to the throne she had known under her father, reflecting a heart still measuring worth by worldly status rather than devotion to God.

views.label: A foil highlighting true, uninhibited worship
views.case: The verse is placed immediately after the description of joyful, wholehearted celebration precisely so that Michal's cold judgment stands out in contrast, showing that not everyone who observes true worship shares in it, and that wholehearted devotion to God will sometimes be despised by those who prize appearances.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the verse functions as a deliberate contrast to the chapter's joy, closing this movement with a warning that even right worship may draw contempt from those unwilling to join it.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter turns the failure of chapter 13 into careful obedience: the aron is finally nasa, carried, by the Levites' own shoulders on poles rather than a cart, exactly as Moses commanded. Before they may touch the poles, the priests and Levites must qadash themselves, be consecrated, made holy for holy work, David's explicit correction of the earlier carelessness. And the Levites are named as those set apart to sharat, minister, before the ark forever, the same word that will describe Asaph's ongoing ministry of music and thanksgiving in the very next chapter, so that consecration and service flow directly into worship and song.

typology.type: The consecrated Levites carrying the ark on their shoulders
typology.fulfillment: Christ, our great High Priest, perfectly consecrated to bear God's presence to His people without danger, Hebrews 4:14-16.

typology.type: The appointed singers ministering continually before the ark
typology.fulfillment: The church's ongoing sacrifice of praise offered continually through Christ, Hebrews 13:15, and believers as a royal priesthood, 1 Peter 2:5, 9.

study.christ: This chapter's careful consecration and joyful, ordered worship anticipates Christ, the truly and perfectly consecrated High Priest who alone can bear God's presence to His people safely and forever, Hebrews 4:14-16. Where the Levites had to be set apart, qadash, before approaching the ark, believers are made holy once for all through Christ's own sacrifice, Hebrews 10:10, and are now invited, as a royal priesthood, to offer through Him a continual sacrifice of praise, the very ministry of thanksgiving and music this chapter establishes, Hebrews 13:15 and 1 Peter 2:5, 9.
study.god: God is holy, and His holiness requires that those who approach Him be consecrated and that His revealed order be followed, not improvised. Yet the same chapter reveals a God who delights in joy, music, sacrifice, and uninhibited celebration when His people seek Him rightly; holiness and gladness are not opposites in Scripture but partners, and this procession, corrected and careful, overflows with both.

crossReferences.note: The Kohathites may carry the holy things only by their poles, never touching them directly, the command finally obeyed in this chapter.
The sons of Kohath were given no carts because their charge was to carry the holy things on their shoulders.
The parallel account of this same procession, including David's dance and Michal's contempt, with additional detail about the sacrifices offered along the way.
Christ our great High Priest, who alone can bring us confidently into God's presence.
Believers are called to offer through Christ a continual sacrifice of praise, echoing the Levites' appointed ministry of music here.

study.oneStory: Where chapter 13 ended in judgment because Israel sought God's presence on its own terms, this chapter shows what happens when His people are consecrated, careful, and obedient to His revealed order: joy, music, sacrifice, and triumphant procession. For the exiles rebuilding worship after generations of loss, and for every reader since, this is the pattern of restored worship, one that finds its full and final fulfillment in Christ, the perfectly consecrated High Priest who forever brings His people safely into the joy of God's presence.

quiz.q: What specific correction did David make from chapter 13's failed attempt?

options.text: He had the consecrated Levites carry the ark on their shoulders with poles, as Moses had commanded, instead of using a cart.
options.note: Verses 2, 13, and 15 name this exact correction, following Numbers 4:15 and 7:9.

options.text: He waited a full year before attempting to move the ark again.
options.note: No such waiting period is recorded; the text moves directly from preparation to the corrected procession.

options.text: He assembled a larger crowd to celebrate the procession.
options.note: A large assembly is present, but the decisive correction the text highlights is the method of carrying the ark, not crowd size.

options.text: He appointed priests to offer sacrifices before setting out.
options.note: Sacrifices are indeed offered, verse 26, but the chapter's explicit, named correction is that the Levites now carry the ark by its poles rather than by cart.

quiz.q: What did David say had caused the LORD's anger the first time, in verse 13?

options.text: That the Levites were not carrying the ark and Israel had not consulted the LORD about the proper order.
options.note: Verse 13 gives David's own explicit diagnosis of the earlier failure.

options.text: That Uzzah personally was an unworthy man.
options.note: David's stated explanation concerns the nation's failure to follow the proper order, not a personal accusation against Uzzah.

options.text: That the sacrifices offered were insufficient in number.
options.note: The issue named is procedural, about the proper order of carrying the ark, not the quantity of sacrifices.

options.text: That the ark had been kept too long at Kiriath-jearim before being moved.
options.note: This is not the reason David gives; his stated diagnosis is specifically about not consulting the LORD regarding the proper order of transport.

quiz.q: What role did the appointed singers and musicians play in this procession?

options.text: They were appointed by name, under Chenaniah's skilled direction, to lift up their voices with joy on harps, lyres, and cymbals as the ark was carried up.
options.note: Verses 16-24 name the singers, musicians, and their director in detail.

options.text: They were forbidden from playing instruments near the ark out of reverence.
options.note: The opposite is true; musicians were specifically appointed to play instruments as part of the procession.

options.text: Only the priests, not the Levites, were permitted to sing before the ark.
options.note: The named singers, such as Heman, Asaph, and Ethan, were Levites, not exclusively priests.

options.text: Music was added only after the ark safely reached Jerusalem.
options.note: Music was part of the procession itself, appointed before and during the journey, not added only upon arrival.

quiz.q: What did David and the Levites wear during the procession, and what does it suggest?

options.text: Robes of fine linen, and David also wore a linen ephod, marking the occasion as a consecrated, priestly act of worship.
options.note: Verse 27 records this clothing, signaling the sacred, priestly character of the whole procession.

options.text: Battle armor, showing the procession was treated as a military operation.
options.note: The clothing described is priestly linen, not military armor, fitting the worshipful nature of the event.

options.text: Ordinary everyday clothing with no particular significance.
options.note: The specific mention of fine linen and a linen ephod indicates deliberate, priestly significance, not ordinary dress.

options.text: Royal robes reserved only for David as king.
options.note: The linen was worn by David and the Levites and singers alike, not as an exclusively royal garment, emphasizing shared, consecrated worship rather than royal distinction.

quiz.q: How did Michal respond to David's worship as the ark entered the city?

options.text: She watched from a window and despised him in her heart for dancing and celebrating.
options.note: Verse 29 records this response directly, a note of contempt closing an otherwise joyful chapter.

options.text: She joined David in the dancing and celebration.
options.note: The text records the opposite; she despised him rather than joining the celebration.

options.text: She rebuked the Levites for their loud music.
options.note: Her contempt in this chapter is directed at David's dancing, as recorded; the following chapter's parallel account, 2 Samuel 6, records her confronting David directly, not the Levites.

options.text: She was afraid of the ark's power and hid from the procession.
options.note: The text specifically says she watched from a window and despised David; fear of the ark is not the reaction described.

questions: David did not just try harder the second time, he did the careful, humble work of consulting God's revealed order. Where might you need to slow down and ask what God has actually said, rather than simply trying again with more effort?

The Levites had to consecrate themselves before they could carry the ark. What would it look like for you to prepare your heart deliberately before an act of worship, rather than approaching it casually?

This corrected procession overflowed with music, sacrifice, and uninhibited joy. Where in your own worship life might careful obedience actually be the doorway to greater joy, rather than a restriction on it?

Michal despised David's wholehearted worship even as everyone else rejoiced. Have you ever felt judged, or tempted to judge someone else, for worshiping God with more freedom or emotion than seemed proper?

The Chronicler names ordinary people, singers, gatekeepers, guardians, by name, for their part in this worship. How does it change your sense of your own small role in a church or community to know that God notices and records such details?

translationNotes.note: Gershomites is a BSB-noted variant of Gershonites, the more familiar spelling used elsewhere, 1 Chronicles 23:7.
The source text lists the instruments after a dash, 'instruments, harps, lyres, and cymbals'; the em dash has been converted to a comma here with no change of sense.

Several Hebrew manuscripts and most Septuagint manuscripts read differently at points in this verse's list of names; the BSB footnotes these textual variants, which do not affect the overall sense of the roster.
The source text sets off 'the priests' with a dash around the list of names; the em dashes have been converted to commas here with no change of sense.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Chronicles 15:1-29. Two em dashes in the source, at verses 16 and 24, were converted to commas; no dashes remain in this file. Sermon search covered Alistair Begg, Tim Keller, John Piper, Sinclair Ferguson, and D.A. Carson for treatments specifically on 1 Chronicles 15; no verified sermon by any of the five was found, so sermon status is none.
